What MotionCue is

MotionCue is a calm companion for people who sit all day. It cues short, expert-sourced stretches through your workday, shows you exactly how to do each one, and lives on both your iPhone and your Apple Watch.

The idea that explains the whole app is this: MotionCue is built to feel like a quiet place, not a checklist. It never nags. It suggests a small stretch now and then, tells you when the next one is due, and lets you make the whole thing yours: your body areas, your session length, your reminders, and a look you find calming. Everything else gets out of the way.

Two devices, one app. Your iPhone is where you set things up and see your progress. Your Apple Watch is the quick "do a stretch now" surface. They stay in sync, so a stretch you finish on your wrist shows up on your phone, and the look you pick on your phone travels to your watch.

Getting around

On the iPhone, everything lives behind four tabs along the bottom.

  • Home is your day: today's stretches, when the next one is due, and a quick water tracker.
  • Library is the full menu of stretches, grouped by body area, if you want to browse or try one on the spot.
  • Progress is your streak, your minutes, and a calendar of the days you showed up.
  • Settings is where you make MotionCue yours: focus areas, session length, reminders, water goals, and the theme.

You can tap between them freely; nothing is lost when you leave a screen.

Your first launch

The first time you open MotionCue, two quick screens come before the app.

  1. A health acknowledgment. MotionCue is general wellness, not medical advice. You confirm you have read a short safety note before anything starts.
  2. Choosing a plan. MotionCue is a paid app with one simple subscription: pick Annual (the best value) or Monthly, tap Subscribe, and confirm with your Apple ID. There is one subscription and it includes everything: every stretch, every theme, the watch app, all of it. Nothing is a separate purchase.

After that you are in, and you land on Home. If you ever reinstall or move to a new device, tap Restore Purchases on the plan screen to bring your subscription back.

Why cited stretches? The plan screen names the reason MotionCue exists: every move is sourced to a real authority (Mayo Clinic, Cleveland Clinic, Harvard, the American Physical Therapy Association), so you are not following an app's guess. You will see those citations throughout.

Your Home screen

Home is designed to be glanceable. From the top down: a greeting, a calm scene, a small "how you are doing" card, and today's stretches.

The greeting and the scene

Up top is a warm "Good morning / Good afternoon / Good evening," today's date, and a small tag showing your current theme. Below it is a calm illustrated scene with a seated figure in it. That figure is Stanley, MotionCue's mascot, sitting quietly in whichever theme you have chosen. He is just there to make the app feel like a place, not a dashboard.

The "Today" card

The card under the scene is the heart of Home. It shows, in plain words:

  • How you are doing: your streak and how many sessions you have done today.
  • When you are next up: a line like "Last stretch 20 minutes ago" and "Next 2:06 PM," so you always know where you stand without doing math.
  • Water: a small ring and a plus and minus button to log a glass (more on that below).
  • Begin: a quiet button to start your next session right now. It reads "Begin," or "Repeat" once you have finished today's set.

Today's stretches

Below the card is today's short list of stretches, chosen for you from the body areas you care about. Tap any one to open it: you will see the figure, the plain-English instruction, a form cue, and its source. From there you can start just that stretch, or mark it done.

Tip. The whole point of the "next up" line is that you do not have to remember. Glance at Home, see when your next stretch is due, and get back to work. MotionCue will nudge you when it is time (if you have reminders on).

Doing a stretch

Every stretch in MotionCue is shown, not just described. A small figure demonstrates the exact motion, so you can follow along without reading.

When you tap a stretch, you get three things beyond the figure:

  • A short instruction in plain language: what to do.
  • A form cue: the one thing to get right so it actually helps.
  • The source, shown as "Source: Mayo Clinic" (or Cleveland Clinic, Harvard, or the APTA). Tap it to open the original.

Start one, or run a whole session

You have two ways to actually stretch:

  1. One stretch: open a stretch and tap Start to do just that one, timed.
  2. A full session: tap Begin on the Today card. MotionCue plays through your stretches back to back, with the figure animating each move and a short rest between them.

During a session, a gentle chime and haptic buzz cue you when to switch sides and when a stretch finishes, so you can close your eyes or look away and still follow along. When you are done, a small celebration appears with a quick water check-in.

The buzz works on silent. The haptic cues fire even with your phone on silent, so a quiet office is not a problem. You can turn the chime and haptics off in Settings if you prefer.

Water

Sitting and stretching pair naturally with staying hydrated, so MotionCue keeps a light water tracker right on Home.

On the Today card, the water row shows a small ring filling toward your daily goal and your total so far, like "24 of 64 oz." Two buttons let you log or take back one serving at a time:

  • Tap plus to add a glass.
  • Tap minus if you tapped by accident or over-counted.

In Settings, under Hydration, you can set your units (ounces or milliliters), your daily goal, and optional water reminders. If you turn on Sync with Apple Health, the water you log is saved to Health, and your total also counts water logged by other apps.

Tip. The water accent stays blue in every theme, on purpose, so water always reads as water no matter which look you pick.

Streaks and progress

The Progress tab is the gentle "look how you are doing" view. It is meant to encourage, not to pressure.

At the top is your current streak as a big number, with your longest streak below it. Then three quick totals: sessions, minutes, and how many you have done this week. Below that is a calendar of the current month with the days you showed up filled in, and a "Where you've focused" chart showing which body areas you have worked most.

Before your first session, this tab simply says "No sessions yet: finish your first stretch and your streak starts here."

Rest days never break your streak. If you turn a day off in Settings (say, weekends), that day is transparent: skipping it will not reset your streak.

When you are caught up

Finish everything for the day and Home shifts into a calmer "all caught up" state, without hiding what is next.

Two tidy rows appear:

  • Up next: tap to preview the stretches your next round would hold, so you are never left wondering what is coming.
  • Completed today: tap to see everything you already finished.

Both stay collapsed so the screen feels done rather than cluttered. If you want to keep going, the Begin button becomes Repeat.

The Stretch Library

The Library tab is the full menu, in case you want to explore beyond today's picks or target something specific.

Stretches are grouped by body area (neck, shoulders and upper back, chest and posture, wrists and forearms, lower back and hips, lower body and ankles). Each entry shows its name, a one-line instruction, how long it takes, and its source. Tap Try on any stretch to do it right then, using the same timed player as a session.

Every stretch is sourced. The whole library is built from published guidance, and the citation travels with each stretch here and everywhere else you meet it.

Make it yours: themes

MotionCue ships with eight calm themes, each a small change of palette and scenery. They are all included; picking one is purely about what feels good to you.

The full set is Neutral (the calm default), Nagomi (the featured theme), Classic (the original green), Aegean, Lagom, Hygge, Toscana, and Marrakech. Six of them carry their own illustrated scene behind Stanley.

How to switch

  1. Open Settings.
  2. Under Theme, tap any look. A checkmark shows your current one, and the whole app, including your watch, updates.
Meet Stanley. The calm figure on your Home is Stanley, sitting cross-legged and gazing into each theme's scene. He is the app's friendly face; the separate, thinner figure you saw earlier is the one that demonstrates each actual stretch.

On your wrist: Apple Watch

MotionCue's Apple Watch app is the fastest way to do a stretch without picking up your phone. It mirrors your phone's calm look, right down to Stanley and your theme.

Raise your wrist and you see the same warm greeting, Stanley in your chosen theme, a small progress bar, and your list of stretches. Tap any stretch to do it on the watch, with the figure and a timer. A gentle buzz cues you along the way, and when a stretch finishes you can tap Stretch again to keep going. Everything you do on the watch flows back to your phone.

Your settings come along. The areas, session length, schedule, and theme you set on the phone all sync to the watch automatically. You set up on the phone; you stretch on either.

Settings: making it yours

Settings is where MotionCue becomes your MotionCue. Every section is plain and does one thing.

  • Focus areas: turn body areas on or off. Your sessions pull only from the areas you keep on.
  • Theme: pick your look, as above.
  • Session length: a slider from one to fifteen minutes. Each session fills that time with as many stretches as fit.
  • During a session: toggle the Chime and haptics cues on or off.
  • Schedule: tap the day circles to choose your active days, then set your Reminders style (sound and vibration, vibration only, or off), how often, and your quiet hours.
  • Hydration: units, daily goal, water reminders, and optional Apple Health sync.
  • Health & Safety: re-read the safety note anytime.
  • About: your app version, a "Check for updates" button, and a Help & FAQ link to the MotionCue website.

Reminders, in plain terms

If reminders are on, MotionCue nudges you to stretch on a rolling cadence (every 30 to 120 minutes, your choice) but only during the hours and on the days you allow. Set Quiet from and Quiet until to protect your evenings; a reminder that would land inside quiet hours simply waits for the next open window.

A note on safety

MotionCue is built to be gentle and honest, and it wants you to be careful.

General wellness, not medical advice. MotionCue is not a substitute for a doctor or a physical therapist. Consult your physician before starting any new physical activity. If a stretch hurts, ease off or skip it. You confirmed this acknowledgment when you first opened the app, and you can re-read it anytime under Settings, Health & Safety.

Because every stretch is sourced to a named authority, you can always check the original guidance yourself: just tap the citation. That is the point. MotionCue would rather show its work than ask you to trust it.

Questions? The in-app Help & FAQ lives at motioncue.fit/faq.html. Stretch well.
